title: ‘Love Lies Bleeding’ and the 21st Century Noir Movie Canon
format: Sean and Amanda run the coming-attractions wire, review Rose Glass's 'Love Lies Bleeding,' pick a 10-film canon of 21st-century noir, and close with Sean's interview with Glass.

films.title: Dune: Part Two
films.note: Held up as proof that large-format is now the center of the business, and the reason the hosts are giddy that Paul Thomas Anderson's untitled $100M Leonardo DiCaprio movie (dated for August 8, 2025) will play in IMAX.

films.title: Eddington
films.note: Ari Aster's original Western, confirmed this week with Joaquin Phoenix, Pedro Pascal, Emma Stone, and Austin Butler. Amanda, no Western fan, still buys in on the cast.

films.title: Venom: The Last Dance
films.note: The third Venom, re-dated to October, where Sean insists the horror-adjacent symbiote belongs. He spends several minutes explaining the premise to Amanda and calling the title chef's kiss; his thesis is that Venom is as dumb-smart as it thinks it is, unlike Deadpool.

films.title: Venom
films.note: The first one, which came out the same weekend as 'A Star Is Born' and became a running Amanda bit. Michelle Williams plays Eddie Brock's forlorn ex; Sean reads the whole thing as an allegory for a toxic relationship.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Invoked only as the movie Amanda was riding the wave of the weekend Venom opposite it.

films.title: The Batman Part II
films.note: Bumped to 2026 in the Warner Bros. reshuffle. Sean liked the first one and Matt Reeves; the two of them wonder who the villain is and whether Colin Farrell's Penguin (whose spinoff show Sean has been cutting) factors in.

films.title: Horizon: An American Saga - Chapter 2
films.note: Amanda's milestone-birthday movie, arriving the day after. She is told to savor it.

films.title: Love Lies Bleeding
films.note: Kristen Stewart, full mullet, as a New Mexico gym clerk who falls for a bodybuilder (Katie O'Brien) passing through; Ed Harris, bald on top with long hair down the back, is the violent father she's hiding from. Sean reads it as a fairly faithful noir with one big exception: a literalized Attack-of-the-50-Foot-Woman finish he loves and compares to the levitation in 'Saint Maud.' Both hosts rate the physicality, gore, and Stewart-O'Brien chemistry; both find the last third, once Jackie goes to Vegas and turns into a steroid monster, hard to follow.

segments.heading: The 21st Century Noir Canon
segments.note: the final 10, in the order they read it

films.title: Shutter Island
films.note: Scorsese; Sean calls it one of the great underrated movies of the last 25 years, noir dress-up staged inside a delusional mind, with DiCaprio imagining himself as a Bogart-esque detective.

films.title: Mulholland Drive
films.note: Lynch; a no-brainer for Sean, a surreal noir about fame, love, and identity that's also a gay love story.

films.title: Brick
films.note: Rian Johnson's debut; too overt an homage to leave off, a Humphrey Bogart movie relocated to a high school.

films.title: Decision to Leave
films.note: Park Chan-wook; pays homage to the genre while subverting expectations of it. Amanda's pick to include.

films.title: Gone Girl
films.note: Fincher; the most conventional entry (Affleck the confused dipshit, Rosamund Pike the femme fatale) and the one that turns the conventions inside out. Sets off a long Kate Middleton tangent about 'gone-girling' a husband.

films.title: Inherent Vice
films.note: Paul Thomas Anderson; true to the spirit and updating it via the stoner-PI haze. Sean notes it spawned a major fight in his marriage.

films.title: Killer Joe
films.note: William Friedkin, written by Tracy Letts. Sets up a tangent about Letts's wife Carrie Coon and her 10,000-Blu-ray physical-media collection.

films.title: Memento
films.note: Nolan; picked for what it signaled: the tonality he'd carry into 'The Dark Knight' and 'Inception,' which everyone else started chasing.

films.title: Sexy Beast
films.note: Jonathan Glazer's debut; a sunny, Spain-set 'retiree noir.'

films.title: Drive
films.note: Nicolas Winding Refn; the example, the one Sean added first. Kicks off an appreciation of the Ryan Gosling renaissance.

films.title: Nightmare Alley
films.note: Del Toro's remake of the 1947 noir, sold in black-and-white; the traditional-but-absurd carnival noir the hosts break down (per the earlier Patton Oswalt episode) as a template for the genre.

films.title: Phoenix
films.note: The Petzold film, on Sean's shortlist and a movie he loves.

films.title: The Place Beyond the Pines
films.note: Cianfrance; part of the Gosling stretch Sean cites as noir-adjacent.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: Spike Lee; one of Sean's favorite movies, a fit that ultimately didn't make the 10.

films.title: Oldboy
films.note: Park Chan-wook; Sean says it kind of sort of fits.

films.title: Widows
films.note: Steve McQueen; one of Sean's favorite movies of the last 10 years, its second mention on the show that week.

films.title: A Touch of Sin
films.note: Jia Zhangke; on the shortlist Sean reads out.

films.title: Looper
films.note: One of the 'how could you not put in' near-misses.

films.title: Under the Silver Lake
films.note: Another of the 'how could you not put in' near-misses.

films.title: Mystic River
films.note: Floated as a Sean Penn entry that didn't make it.

films.title: Match Point
films.note: Woody Allen; raised in the same breath as 'Mystic River' and left off.

films.title: Love Lies Bleeding
films.note: Glass co-wrote it with Veronica and set it in 1989 New Mexico to strip out phones and heighten the isolation; the ending grew out of the surreal bodily elements teased throughout, filmed against tennis balls and a digital Ed Harris. She sees Lou, Jackie, and Maud as spiritual sisters and wanted a romance that's parasitic rather than redemptive.

films.title: Saint Maud
films.note: Her isolating, one-location debut, which she credits for how they pulled it off; Ed Harris's wife Amy was a fan, which helped land him. The 50-foot finish echoes Maud's ambiguous levitation.

films.title: Jason and the Argonauts
films.note: The Harryhausen skeletons she saw young, the 'how did they do that' spark that got her into stop-motion, then filmmaking.

films.title: Spencer
films.note: The Oscar-nominated role Kristen Stewart came off just before signing on, when 'she can do whatever she wants.'

films.title: Love Me
films.note: Stewart's other Sundance film, which Glass caught there and calls 'so odd and lovely.'

films.title: Eno
films.note: The Brian Eno documentary that self-generates a different edit every screening, the Sundance standout that has Glass thinking about non-traditional, immersive formats.

films.title: Bigger Than Life
films.note: Nicholas Ray's James Mason drug parable that Sean pairs with 'Attack of the 50-Foot Woman' as the homage he reads in the ending; Glass hasn't seen it.
